content: HYPERLIQUID — On-Chain Perpetual Futures: Layer-1 application-specific chain replicating CEX latency with fully on-chain order books. 24/7 cross-asset trading without intermediaries. Key advantages: No KYC, self-custody, transparent order book, lower fees than most CEXs. Risk: Smart contract risk, bridge risk, less liquid than Binance. Best for: Traders who want on-chain execution with CEX-like speed. Supports up to 50× leverage on major pairs. Insurance fund backstops liquidations.

HYPERLIQUID LIQUIDATION LOGIC — Partial Close-Out: Unlike CEXs that often fully liquidate, Hyperliquid prioritizes partial close-outs in cross-margin mode. The engine attempts to close only a fraction of the largest losing position to restore maintenance margin before total account wipeout. This preserves user capital during flash crashes. When full liquidation is required, the HLP vault absorbs the toxic flow. ADL (Auto-Deleveraging) is the last resort — forcibly closes profitable traders' positions only if insurance fund is depleted.

AUTO-DELEVERAGING (ADL) — Systemic Failsafe: ADL socializes losses by forcibly closing highly profitable traders' positions when the insurance fund is depleted during black-swan events. This prevents protocol insolvency. ADL ranking: Traders are ranked by leverage × profit. Highest-ranked (most leveraged, most profitable) get deleveraged first. How to avoid ADL: Use lower leverage, take partial profits, don't be in the top 1% of profitable traders during extreme moves. ADL is rare but devastating — it's the cost of on-chain trading without a central authority to absorb losses.

UNISWAP V3 CONCENTRATED LIQUIDITY: LPs deposit capital into custom price ranges instead of 0 to infinity. This boosts capital efficiency 3-5× vs traditional AMMs. Example: Deposit USDC/ETH in ±5% range around current price = 20× more fee revenue than full-range. Risk: If price moves OUT of your range, you earn zero fees and hold 100% of the underperforming asset. Mitigation: 70-80% in correlated pairs (USDC/USDT, WBTC/ETH) with tight ranges. 20-30% in volatile pairs with wider ranges. Rebalance when price breaches tick bounds.

IMPERMANENT LOSS (IL) MITIGATION: IL = opportunity cost when deposited asset ratio diverges from entry. Concentrated liquidity AMPLIFIES IL. Mitigation strategies: 1) Correlated pairs only (USDC/USDT = near-zero IL). 2) Wide ranges for volatile pairs (±20% instead of ±5%). 3) Automated rebalancing via Gelato Network scripts. 4) Layer 2 deployment (Arbitrum) to minimize gas costs of rebalancing. 5) Fee revenue must EXCEED IL — if not, you're better off holding. Rule: Only LP in pairs where you'd be comfortable holding both assets anyway.

DELTA-NEUTRAL OPTIONS — DDH on Deribit: Delta Dynamic Hedging (DDH) = continuously calculating portfolio Delta and executing offsetting spot/perp trades to zero out directional exposure. By maintaining delta-neutral book, you lock in Theta (time decay) and Vega (volatility premium) while being agnostic to price direction. Tools: Greeks Live Auto DDH via WebSocket API. Implementation: 1) Sell options to collect premium. 2) Calculate aggregate delta. 3) Hedge with spot or perp. 4) Rebalance as delta shifts. Risk: Gamma risk — large sudden moves can blow through hedge.

OPTIONS GREEKS — Risk Sensitivities: Delta: Expected price change per $1 move in underlying (-1 to 1). ATMs have ~0.5 delta. Gamma: Rate of change of Delta. Peaks ATM. Highest risk — violent moves away from strike cause convexity losses. Theta: Time decay. Options lose value daily. Sellers collect Theta. Vega: Sensitivity to volatility changes. High Vega = high sensitivity to vol expansion/contraction. For directional traders: Buy options when Vega is cheap (low IV), sell when expensive (high IV). Use Delta to size positions. Monitor Gamma for tail risk.

LAYER 2 GAS OPTIMIZATION FOR LP REBALANCING: On Ethereum mainnet, rebalancing a Uniswap V3 position costs $20-100+ in gas. This erodes fee revenue, especially for smaller positions. Solution: Deploy on Arbitrum, Optimism, or Base. Gas costs drop to $0.10-2.00 per rebalance. Automated rebalancing scripts (Gelato Network) weigh compounded fee revenue against gas costs — only rebalance when expected fee gain > gas cost. Rule: If position <$10K, MUST use L2. If >$100K, mainnet is viable but L2 is still optimal.

HYPE TOKEN — Deflationary Fee Burn Model: Hyperliquid's native asset HYPE functions as a direct economic sink, actively burning supply through trading fee conversions. This is analogous to corporate equity buybacks — reducing supply increases per-token value. The burn rate is proportional to trading volume. Higher volume = more burns = more deflationary pressure. HYPE also provides governance rights and staking rewards. Investment thesis: HYPE value is directly tied to Hyperliquid trading volume growth. If Hyperliquid captures CEX market share, HYPE appreciates structurally.

ON-CHAIN ORDER BOOK VS AMM — When to Use: Order Book (Hyperliquid, dYdX): Better for large orders, precise execution, limit orders, market making. Higher liquidity, lower slippage on major pairs. Requires active management. AMM (Uniswap, Curve): Better for passive yield, long-tail assets, permissionless listing. Set-and-forget liquidity provision. Higher slippage on large orders. Rule: Use order books for active trading. Use AMMs for passive yield on assets you'd hold anyway. Hybrid: Use AMMs for small-cap discovery, order books for position management.
